title: Minor scale
content:
In music theory, the term minor scale refers to three scale patterns – the natural minor scale (or Aeolian mode), the harmonic minor scale, and the melodic minor scale (ascending or descending) – rather than just one as with the major scale. \override Score.TimeSignature #'stencil = ##f \relative c' {\clef treble \time 7/4 c4^\markup {C natural minor scale} d es f g aes bes c2 </score \override Score.TimeSignature #'stencil = ##f \relative c' {\clef treble \time 7/4 c4^\markup {C harmonic minor scale} d es f g aes b!?

c2 </score \override Score.TimeSignature #'stencil = ##f \relative c' {\clef treble \time 7/4 c4^\markup {C melodic minor scale} d es f g a!? b!? c bes aes g f es d c2 </score In each of these scales, the first, third, and fifth <a href="scale%20degree" scale degree</a s form a <a href="minor%20triad" minor triad</a (rather than a <a href="Major%20chord" major triad</a, as in a major scale).

In some contexts, "minor scale" is used to refer to any <a href="heptatonic%20scale" heptatonic scale</a with this property (see <a href="Minor%20scale%23Related%20modes" Related modes</a below). Natural minor scale. Relationship to relative major.

A natural minor scale (or <a href="Aeolian%20mode" Aeolian mode</a) is a <a href="diatonic%20scale" diatonic scale</a that is built by starting on the sixth <a href="Degree%20%28music%29" degree</a of its <a href="relative%20major" relative</a <a href="major%20scale" major scale</a.

For instance, the A natural minor scale can be built by starting on the 6th degree of the C major scale: \override Score.TimeSignature #'stencil = ##f \relative c' {\clef treble \time 7/4 c4^\markup {C major scale} d e f g \override NoteHead.color = #red a \override NoteHead.color = #black b c2 \bar "||" \time 9/4 </score Because of this, the key of A minor is called the "relative minor" of C major. Every major key has a relative minor, which starts on the 6th scale degree or step.

For instance, since the 6th degree of F major is D, the relative minor of F major is D minor. Relationship to parallel major. A natural minor scale can also be constructed by altering a major scale with accidentals. In this way, a natural minor scale is represented by the following notation: Each degree of the scale, starting with the tonic (the first, lowest note of the scale), is represented by a number. Their difference from the major scale is shown. Thus, a number without a flat represents a major (or perfect) interval. A number with a flat represents a minor interval.
